method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The modern Icelandic name of the country is Ísland, which is pronounced roughly “Ee-sland,” and in many other languages the name is associated with “island” rather than “ice.”[2] The commonly accepted explanation is that the name was given by Norse settlers, although some traditions and theories have suggested alternative origins.[2][4] Early settlement accounts preserved in Íslendingabók and Landnámabók attribute the naming of the land to Norse explorers, including Naddodd, who called it Snæland, and Garðar Svavarsson, who named it Garðarshólmur.[2][4] The name Ísland is generally linked to Hrafna-Flóki Vilgerðarson, whose description of the country as “ice land” is said to have produced the lasting form of the name.[4] By the time of later medieval traditions, Ísland had already become the established name in regular use.[4]

==History==
Iceland was settled during the Viking Age, traditionally beginning with Ingólfur Arnarson’s arrival in 874, and the island was populated by a mixed Norse and Celtic community that developed a distinctive society and legal tradition. [3][4] The medieval Icelandic commonwealth became known for its sagas and as one of the earliest democratic polities in Europe, while the settlement period also helped set the pattern for later voyages from Iceland to Greenland and Vinland. [3]  

Iceland’s history has been closely shaped by its geology, with eruptive activity, earthquakes, and geothermal forces recurring throughout the historical record. [2][3] Written accounts of eruptions from the 12th and 13th centuries, combined with tephra layers, lava flows, and vent structures, have made it possible to reconstruct Iceland’s volcanic history with unusual precision. [2] Researchers have identified 205 historical volcanic events, including long-lived eruptions that ranged widely in size and volume. [2]  

Among the most significant eruptions were the Eldgjá eruption of 934–940 and the Laki eruption of 1783–1784, both in South Iceland, which are regarded as the two largest volcanic events in Iceland’s recorded history and among the largest of their kind globally in the last two millennia. [2] Iceland’s frequent earthquakes have generally caused limited damage in the modern period, in part because buildings constructed since the mid-20th century have typically been designed to withstand strong seismic shocks. [3]  

The island’s historical development also reflects long-term interaction between volcanic activity and glaciation. [2][4] Geological studies have shown that Iceland’s landscape has been repeatedly reshaped by both volcanism and ice, with the oldest exposed rocks dating to only 14–16 million years ago despite the much older activity of the Iceland mantle plume. [2]

==Geography==
Iceland is a volcanic island in the North Atlantic Ocean, located between the Greenland Sea and the North Atlantic and just south of the Arctic Circle. It is Europe’s westernmost country and, by area, is slightly smaller than the U.S. state of Kentucky.[2][5]

The island lies astride the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, where the Eurasian and North American plates diverge, and this tectonic setting is responsible for its frequent earthquakes, eruptions, geysers, and geothermal activity. The country’s surface is shaped by volcanic and glacial processes, producing lava fields, volcanoes, glaciers, waterfalls, black sand beaches, and rugged coastlines.[1][3][4][5]

Much of Iceland consists of plateaus, mountains, and ice-covered highlands, while its coasts are cut by fjords and other deep inlets carved by glaciers. The Central Highlands are largely uninhabited and include extensive volcanic desert terrain.[2][4][5]

Glaciers cover about one-tenth to slightly more than one-tenth of the country, with the largest ice caps concentrated in the central highlands. These ice masses are slowly retreating as climate conditions change.[1][2][3][5]

Land use is limited by the harsh physical environment: only a small share of the country is suitable for grazing and pasture, and cultivation occupies an even smaller portion. Birch forests are scarce, although reforestation efforts are underway.[1]

Iceland’s geography also provides important geothermal resources, with high subsurface temperatures at relatively shallow depths in many areas. This has made geothermal energy a significant national asset.[3][4]

==Politics==
Iceland is a constitutional republic with a multi-party parliamentary system in which the president serves as head of state and executive power is exercised by the government.[3][4] Legislative power is vested in the 63-member Althingi, one of the world’s oldest parliamentary assemblies, whose members are elected by direct popular vote to four-year terms.[3][4] The president is elected directly by the people for a four-year term and generally plays a largely ceremonial role, although the office formally participates in government formation after elections.[2][3] Following parliamentary elections, the president عادة appoints the leader of the largest party, or another party leader if necessary, to try to form a cabinet that can command parliamentary support.[3][4] The cabinet remains in office until the next election or until it loses the confidence of parliament and resigns.[3][4]  

The judiciary is independent of the executive and legislature and consists of the Supreme Court, a court of appeal, and district courts.[3][4] Local government is organized through municipalities, which handle matters such as primary education, municipal services, and some social programs.[4]  

Although Iceland’s modern state was established only in 1944, its politics have been shaped by the long struggle for self-government and by the country’s older parliamentary traditions.[1][3] In the contemporary period, the 2008 financial crisis had a major effect on political life, contributing to changes in party competition and public trust.[1] Since then, new political parties have gained prominence and coalition patterns have shifted, while rural-urban and left-right divisions remain important political cleavages.[1] Iceland continues to be regarded as a high-performing democracy by international measures, though recent years have seen declines in economic equality and freedom of expression, including academic freedom.[1]

==Economy==
Iceland has a small but advanced economy built around tourism, fishing, and aluminum smelting, with these sectors shaping both exports and employment.[2] The country’s limited domestic market has encouraged a strong outward orientation in trade and investment.[2]

Fishing and marine products remain a core part of the economy, with fish and fish products among Iceland’s major exports and the industry still highly important to overall output.[1][2] The economy is also vulnerable to changes in fish stocks and to price movements in key export commodities such as aluminum and ferrosilicon.[1]

Since 2010, tourism has been a major driver of economic growth, and in recent years it has accounted for a large share of export earnings.[1][2] Visitor numbers fell sharply during the COVID-19 pandemic, but the sector has since recovered strongly, with tourism again supporting growth and investment in hotels and high-end travel services.[2]

Iceland’s economy has also diversified into manufacturing and service industries, especially software production and biotechnology, while abundant geothermal and hydropower resources have attracted foreign investment in aluminum and data-center-related activity.[1] This energy base has helped support industrial development linked to relatively low-cost renewable electricity.[1]

Recent economic performance has been volatile, with strong growth in 2022 and 2023 followed by a mild contraction in 2024, after a sharp downturn in 2020 caused by the pandemic.[2] Inflation rose markedly during the recovery period but has eased somewhat, while unemployment has remained low by international standards.[2]

In the labor market, Iceland relies on foreign workers to fill many unskilled and semi-skilled jobs, reflecting both labor shortages and the country’s small population.[2] SMEs also play an important role in turnover, alongside a relatively decentralized business structure.[4]

Iceland’s innovation profile is comparatively strong, particularly in public-private co-publications, international scientific collaboration, and foreign doctorate student participation.[4] At the same time, weaknesses remain in exports of medium- and high-tech products and in some design-related indicators.[4]

==Demographics==
Iceland had a population of 402,329 in 2026, with a yearly growth rate of 1.02% and a global rank of 178 by population size.[1] The country’s median age was 36.6 years in 2026.[1]

At the beginning of 2024, Iceland’s population was just under 400,000, with women comprising a slight minority of the total population and men making up just over half.[3] Foreign-born residents and people with foreign backgrounds accounted for about one-fifth of the population, with Poles forming the largest immigrant group.[3]

Iceland’s population has risen rapidly in recent decades, reaching 200,000 in 1968 and 300,000 in 2007, and growth has been supported increasingly by immigration since the mid-2000s.[5] After a short period of net emigration following the 2008 financial crisis, net immigration resumed, and by early 2024 the population was at its highest recorded level.[5]

The 2020 population was estimated at 341,049, with a United Nations projection of 341,243 for the same year, reflecting continued growth from previous years.[2] A 2023 health profile reported a population of 387,558 and provided indicators for population growth, age distribution, and projected demographic change through 2050.[4]

Historically, Iceland’s population remained relatively small and was highly sensitive to epidemics, crop failures, volcanic eruptions, and severe cold, which caused repeated periods in which deaths exceeded births.[5] By the early 21st century, the country had entered a later stage of demographic transition, characterized by low fertility, small families, low mortality, and high life expectancy.[5]

==Culture==
Icelandic culture is shaped by a mix of long-standing traditions, strong state support for cultural institutions, and a contemporary outlook that is increasingly international. [2][4] The government considers it part of its role to create conditions in which artistic variety, creativity, and initiative can flourish, and it supports the arts in order to make cultural participation accessible to everyone in the country. [1] State involvement in the arts focuses mainly on public artistic institutions and support for professional artists, while the Ministry of Culture and Business Affairs is responsible for general cultural affairs. [1]

Cultural heritage is also a major public concern. [1] The authorities support the preservation of historical sites and cultural artefacts as part of passing the nation’s heritage on to future generations, and the Ministry oversees museums and national cultural institutions. [1] Through these institutions, the government also promotes independent research in history and culture and provides the public with opportunities to learn about the past. [1]

Media and broadcasting fall within the same ministerial framework, including the Media Commission and the Icelandic National Broadcasting Service (RÚV). [1] Youth affairs outside formal education are likewise handled by the Ministry, which supports youth organisations and has conducted research in the field since 1992. [1] Sports are also under its remit, including data collection on sporting activity and facilities, support for research, and anti-doping oversight. [1]

Icelandic intellectual and academic life has long been centered on the University of Iceland, which became the country’s main institution for scientific and higher education after earlier theological, medical, and legal schools were merged into it. [2] The university later expanded to include faculties such as philosophy, engineering, and social sciences, reflecting the country’s broader development in scholarship. [2] Research in the physical and social sciences is supported through national funding and international cooperation, with geothermal energy among the notable areas of study. [2]

Popular cultural identity draws on both historical memory and living traditions. [2][4] Nationalist ideas once emphasized the sagas and revived celebrations to reinforce links with the past, while today many Icelanders live in the capital area and participate in a more international culture. [2] At the same time, special days and seasonal feasts continue to play an important social role, including celebrations such as Þorrablót, which features communal meals, singing, games, and storytelling. [4]

Folklore remains especially prominent in Icelandic cultural life. [3][5] Stories about elves, trolls, sea monsters, ghosts, and other supernatural beings are widely associated with Iceland’s landscapes and oral tradition, and they continue to appear in modern cultural writing and popular accounts of the country. [3][5]
